text:
Care and Maintenance of Shibari Rope
Scope
deGiotto says these washing and drying methods apply specifically to its own ropes because manufacturing, material quality, and dye methods vary.
Hemp machine wash
Unbundle the rope and place it in a pillowcase or lingerie bag. Use a delicate cycle, cold water, one-half teaspoon of free-and-clear detergent, and an extra spin cycle if available, then dry and oil as directed below.
Hemp tub wash
Fill a tub or bucket with cool water, add one-half teaspoon of free-and-clear detergent, stir, add the unbundled rope, stir again, leave it for 20 minutes, rinse with cool water, then dry and oil as directed below.
Rainbow or camouflage hemp and jute
Do not immerse multicolor rope because leached dyes and lint can mix and be reabsorbed, muddying the colors. Instead, make the same cool-water detergent solution, hold or anchor one end, and pour the solution through an open hand while walking the rope through it. Rinse the same way with clean water or a hose. Do not let the wet rope touch itself.
Jute tub wash
For deGiotto jute, fill a tub or bucket with cool water, add one-half teaspoon of free-and-clear detergent, stir, add the unbundled rope, stir again, leave it for 20 minutes, rinse with cool water, then dry and treat it as directed below. The vendor says its jute may be wetted but requires the special drying process below.
Silk hand wash
Do not wash silk unless absolutely necessary. Use cool water and one-half teaspoon of Woolite or another delicates detergent, stir gently, do not over-agitate, squeeze, or wring, leave it for five minutes, rinse with cool water, then dry and treat as directed below.
POSH and Hempex wash
Follow the hemp directions and never put POSH or Hempex in a clothes dryer.
Hemp drying
Hang hemp in loose coils until it stops dripping, stretch it fully and tightly, hang it in loose coils until barely damp, stretch it again, then hang in loose coils until completely dry. Drying can take several days; a fan aimed at the rope can speed it.
Jute drying
Wet jute fibers curl, making rope up to 15 percent shorter and fatter. Hang the rope in loose coils until it stops dripping, fully extend and stretch it, then secure both ends so it is firm and at least four feet above the ground. Use twine at the ends rather than attachment knots, whose twists can remain after drying. Hang about one pound at the rope's center and leave it without retightening until completely dry. Drying can take several days; a fan aimed at the rope can speed it. If the rope dries fat and mushy, the vendor says to wet it thoroughly and repeat the process.
Silk drying
Hang silk in loose coils until it stops dripping, stretch it fully and tightly, hang until barely damp, stretch it again, then hang in loose coils until completely dry.
POSH and Hempex drying
Do not store POSH or Hempex wet. The vendor describes these synthetic ropes as usable underwater without their properties changing.
Rainbow or camouflage drying
Follow the applicable hemp or jute drying directions, but do not let the rope touch itself while wet or drying.
Oiling, buttering, and waxing
The vendor says hemp and jute fibers can become brittle over time and after washing. Dust shedding while tying or any wash is its cue to apply oil. Apply only a small amount because excess oil or wax makes rope heavy and sluggish. Do not spray oil directly on rope; spritz an old washcloth and pull the rope through it. Jute may use the vendor's rope butter or oil; hemp may use its rope oil. Silk may be oiled for feel but does not require it for care. POSH and Hempex require none.
title: Care and Maintenance of Shibari Rope
